pi_items.source: setup_task.sh line 9: OUTPUT_FILE matching definition; export_result.sh line 6 confirmation
setup_task.sh line 7: SERIES_DIR='/home/ga/DICOM/ct_cranium'; GitHub v3.0 release contains 0051.zip; DICOM file metadata shows patient name 'CT 0051 - InVesalius Sample'; 108 DICOM slices found

pi_items.verified_value: Anterior-Superior (Superior: top of skull; Anterior: forehead/front)
pi_items.source: Medical literature: Frontal sinuses are 'most superior of the paranasal sinuses' (above bony orbits); Located 'anterior to frontal lobes' (in forehead region); DICOM dataset dimensions confirm anatomical orientation

pi_items.verified_value: Normal frontal sinus: 4000-7000 mm³ (4-7 mL); 500 mm³ is abnormally small (0.5 mL)
pi_items.source: Medical literature: Three-dimensional volumetric analysis and CT measurement studies show normal range 4-7 mL; Individual measurements ~3.9-3.4 mm³ per sinus; Aplasia (absence) 1-15%. Metadata value 500 mm³ appears to be a noise-filtering threshold rather than anatomically expected minimum.

pi_items.verified_value: Normal frontal sinus: 4000-7000 mm³; Hyperplasia ~8% of cases; 50000 mm³ (50 mL) is 7-12x normal maximum
pi_items.source: Medical literature: Normal range well-established at 4-7 mL; Hyperplasia raises volume but typically <12 mL. Dataset analysis shows 108 slices of 512x512 pixels at 0.4785mm resolution over ~160mm Z range, suggesting total air volume much less than 50000 mm³. Metadata value appears to be error margin or includes non-frontal air (nasal cavity, external).

privileged_info_summary: The task uses the official InVesalius v3.0 sample dataset 'CT 0051' containing 108 CT cranium slices (2mm thickness, 512x512 pixels per slice) from release https://github.com/invesalius/invesalius3/releases/download/v3.0/0051.zip. Patient identifier: 'CT 0051 - InVesalius Sample'. The frontal sinus is correctly described as located in the Anterior-Superior region (forehead, above the orbits). Medical literature establishes normal frontal sinus volume at 4,000-7,000 mm³ (4-7 mL), with individual variation. The metadata volume bounds of 500-50,000 mm³ are significantly outside the medically expected range and appear to be heuristic thresholds for the task verifier rather than anatomically valid bounds.
